title: Hazard-ratio / forest-plot reading mini-module
context: Hazard ratios and forest plots appear routinely in cancer trial reporting and patient advocacy materials, but they are rarely explained to non-statistician audiences. This backlog mini-module extends the core curriculum with a short, focused treatment. Coordination with the sibling project 'stats-for-clinicians' is needed to avoid duplicating a clinician-facing treatment of the same topic. The module is statistical-tier (medium risk) and requires biostatistician sign-off. It must implement all editorial guardrail rules, including the absolute-before-relative rule (showing what a hazard ratio means in natural-frequency or absolute-difference terms, not just the ratio).
objective: Author a short mini-module on reading hazard ratios and forest plots, suitable for patient advocates, health journalists, and public health students. The module must explain what a hazard ratio is in plain language with natural-frequency context, how to read a forest plot (including confidence intervals and the null-effect line), and what a 'statistically significant' HR means and doesn't mean. Coordinate with the stats-for-clinicians sibling project to avoid duplication.
acceptanceCriteria: Explains hazard ratio in plain language with a concrete cancer-trial example, showing the absolute-difference context alongside the ratio (absolute-before-relative rule)
Explains how to read a forest plot: point estimate, confidence interval width, the null line, and what crossing the null line means
Explains the difference between statistical significance and clinical significance in the HR context
Coordination note with stats-for-clinicians sibling project confirmed (non-duplication; different audience framing verified)
All statistical assertions cited with aggregate data and data vintage; citation coverage 100%
Carries 'not medical advice' framing; no individualized treatment recommendation implied
Biostatistician/epidemiologist sign-off recorded in the reviewers ledger
output: A short mini-module on reading hazard ratios and forest plots — plain-language, absolute-context framing, coordinated with stats-for-clinicians to avoid duplication, and biostatistician signed off.
